speaker: interviewer
text: --obtaining food from the villagers. Was it primarily your job to obtain the food for the family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Later on, yes. Because they all, I don't know why, but they all talked and said that I didn't look Jewish at the time, those days. And we were going so long-- we were going so long until we found-- for days-- a quarry on a top of a hill, a high hill. I would call it a mountain. And you walk in there, it's just like a big hall that you could even stand up. And-- and that was not far from Ozenin it's called. And we got some straw and we-- so we could be a little more comfortable. And every night I used to go down with my sister and to beg, to beg for some-- for food. And one night that when we went-- one night, when we went down there, they kept telling my sister that she looks Jewish, that they're going to kill her. So she doesn't want to go anymore. And she said to me, I'm not going any more. You go by yourself, because they don't recognize you. The village-- the people in the village, they're very nice. They used to give us food, the Czechoslovakian people. And-- but that particular night when I was going to go all by myself, I looked around. And I saw that my father was following me. He didn't want me to go by my-- by myself.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went. And we could hear-- we could hear some like [INAUDIBLE], like bumps. And-- and then we came back with the food, my father and me, and my mother, my sister, and my brother were dead. My sister was not dead yet. One [INAUDIBLE], one of the sons of these people who were so nice to us throw in a grenade.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's all he got. This was a golden watch for my sister that she had-- that she had gotten as an engagement present before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did your-- did your sister tell you that he was the murderer?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then my sister said to me, don't-- my sister says to me, run away. Because he is going to-- he is going to kill you. He's looking for you. And my father took me by my hand and just rolled me down that hill. And he says, they're all dead. And my sister died in my arms. And he said, you have to survive, so you can tell the story. These were his words. And when I fell down, and it was a terrible thing to leave all this. I didn't want to go. I just want to get finished to. But my father insisted. [INAUDIBLE] [? sister, ?] he rolled me down. I was already on the bottom of the hill. And we started walking. And we stopped in a-- and there were lots of forests over there. And we came to a forest we saw. We saw a-- we saw a little house. And we went in. And there was a Polish lady. And we told her what happened. And she sit down on her knees. And she prayed. She says, praise to God that God should take away some years from her two sons and give me that I should can survive this war. And my father told her that she should take me to a priest to make me Catholic. So he should give me some-- he should give a paper to survive. But he said, well, if you believe in Jesus and you got to heaven, then you go to heaven. I said, if-- if you die, then you go to heaven.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I said, well, if I have to die Catholic and go to heaven, I can just as well die Jewish. So we went away. And he didn't give me any papers to-- to-- that I could hide. And so we went. They were there. And we had to go to bring some food. This lady, that's all she had is an onion. She couldn't give us anything to eat. So I left my father. And I started to walk again for some food. And when I came back, and it was freezing. His toes were frozen. And he left, he walked out. And he walked to the village, which it wasn't very-- very far.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because he said, he had to risk his life. Because he cannot-- if he doesn't have any legs, if it's frozen, then he rather die. And I found him. I looked for him. And I found him in a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I found him in a house-- not in a house, in a-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: In a barn?

speaker: interviewee
text: In a-- what is it called? It's a-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] you know, where the cows are--

speaker: interviewer
text: Barn.

speaker: interviewee
text: In a barn, yes. And I found him in a barn. And his feet were all-- his toes were all black. And on top of this, he got sick with [NON-ENGLISH], That means all his intestines were upside down. His stomach was very big. And-- and he was almost dying.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I used to go from there to the forest. So that Polish lady, she gave me some hot water in a kettle. And I took this. And I came back and to my father. And there was a-- there was a Ukrainian policeman standing right there. And he says, who are you? And I told that I was Jewish. I-- because I-- and he said, where was I? Where did I get the-- [DRINKS] where did I get the kettle with the water? I said that I made a fire. And he says that it's clean. That means I'm lying. And I saw that-- and he said if I tell them who is inside the barn that he will let me go.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said to him, you know, I am so tired of my young life. You'd better shoot me right now. Because I'm not going to tell you who gave me that-- that kettle. And he says, that-- he said that I didn't act Jewish, that I didn't want to save my life by risking some other life. And so he said he would let me go. And it actually was Christmas. And he said that he's not going to shoot me. He's going to shoot-- he said somebody else is going to shoot you anyway. So it was. And afterwards, I went in. And my father was starting to get a little better. And I had to drag him out. At that time, they found lots of Jews in this village, which they took them and they killed them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who?

speaker: interviewee
text: But my father somehow survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is they?

speaker: interviewee
text: They? The Ukrainian, the Ukraine policeman. They round up. Because everybody had the right at this part of the country to shoot a Jew. It's not like in the concentration camps. It was different. Over there, they go to concentration camps. Over here, you can shoot them on the spot. And we just-- he got a little better. And we were-- at one time I was-- yeah, he asked me where I was hiding. I said, where I sleep. I said, I sleep in the barn. Because it was very cold over there. I sleep where the cows are. They breathe. And that was warm. This is how I kept warm. And also, I was hiding in barns where horses are. You know, when you lay down on the floor between the legs on the horses, they're not going to hit you. That's how I used to sleep. And that's what I told that policeman. That's how he-- he saw that I didn't want to tell. So he let me-- he didn't kill me this time. So I survived this time again. Looks like God wanted me to live a little longer. But I actually asked him to kill me. Because it was better be dead than all that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now during all this time, did you just have the one dress and were you still barefoot?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. And when did I get my first shoes? I think it's when the-- the Russians came in. When I was-- but I used to take some rugs, you know? Because it was cold. I had to do something with the-- with my feet. And one day, one time, I was in the barn and a-- no, I think that was later. And my father-- I was in that barn, and a priest, a preacher came in. Evangelist. And he saw me. And-- and he said that maybe I should go by myself. He would take me to his house. And I would work over there. And so he took me to Piatyhory. That wasn't far from there. And I started to work. And I started to work, but-- and I was going to go back to get my father, also, to come there. And when I came, my father was-- he was killed. They said he was killed by the Germans. And there was also, which I forgot to mention that I met one boy, Schlomka. And he was with my father at the time. And he was killed also and my father. Then I was all alone. And I went-- my journey began all by myself. And I didn't even know where they were-- where they were buried. I could never find it. And I worked for this-- at this-- evangelist. I don't know if you call him a preacher, or-- I guess. I was working then. They said that I-- I told them that I was Czechoslovakian, an orphan. And-- but I was work-- the-- the wife recognized that. She said that I don't work like a Czechoslovakian. I work like a Jew. And she told me that I should go to another-- that he send me to another place. Because they were afraid that they-- you know, that they going to-- that they find out.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you think she meant when she said that she thought you worked like a Jew?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because they knew that Jewish people didn't work on farms. That's how she said it. And I went to-- they send me to another. And I became really Evangelist with them. I used to sing when they had the prayers. These were people who spoke with different-- with different languages.

speaker: interviewee
text: So one day they started to-- you know, they scream. And so I started to talk loud. And I said, [NON-ENGLISH]. I said, mother, father, please, help me to survive. And you know, they all started to scream that I am talking in different languages.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I actually-- they went and throw me in the water. And they baptized me. Because they said that I was speaking in their different languages. I-- then I already found out how they real believers. But they were very nice to me. They gave me papers. And I named myself [? Lydia ?] [? Kopezca ?]. [? Kopezca ?] is because that murder who killed my mother and sister and brother, his name was [? Kopezca. ?] So I said, I'm just going to go, you know, I should never in my life forget this name. And the priest, the pre-- preacher told me that somebody-- that he was looking for me. Because he knew that I knew that who killed him. And he so-- he said that he buried me with his own hands, that I was dead, the preacher. So that's-- that was there. And I was just going from-- from then on, they used to send me from one place to the other, not to stay. Send me means we were going to another village to preach. And I've seen even singing solo, [NON-ENGLISH]. But I've forgot the whole language, not a word.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you accompanied this evangelical preacher as he went from town to town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, not [INAUDIBLE]. They used to-- the only people who knew that I was Jewish, the congregation didn't, just the preacher. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he give you false papers also?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Like I said, my name was [? Lydia ?] [? Kopezca ?]. I had them not very long ago that I still had it somewhere. And I misplaced it. I kept it all the years, 50 years. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] then that was 19-- what-- 42, '43 already. And I was in-- in one place. I was in one place that I was singing. And the wife said that her husband is sinning with his thoughts, that he was falling in love with my singing. So she throw me out. She-- not throw me out, she said, I should leave. So I left. I walked and walked and walked. And I came to Zdolbunow. That wasn't very far from Ozenin, where I was. And these villagers that [INAUDIBLE]. And all of a sudden-- that must have been already '44. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Because I saw some soldiers. And they were not Germans, so they were Russians. The war was still on everywhere else. And they said to me, that they said to me-- I told them that I was [? Hebraica ?], that I was Jewish. And they said that I am free. I just couldn't believe that this true. And I thought at that time that I was the only Jewish person in the world. Because all the people I knew were dead. And I just was going from one room, farmhouse, to the other, trying to see if I can find some Jewish people. And they were still bombarding every night. We have to evacuate. They had trains that they took the whole city out from that city. And I was finding the-- I came in in one place where there was a wedding. I saw a pregnant woman. And they performed a wedding. She was marrying that Czechoslovakian person who-- who-- they were-- they were hiding her. And at that wedding, I met my future husband. And we start-- he started the-- to evacuate also. We used to go out every night for this evacuation. And at that time, I met-- I met a-- a couple. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I met a couple who said they want to adopt me, I mean, just to be with them.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so I was. And the husband was starting to get-- how do you say? Fresh with me. So I just left it. And didn't have anywhere to stay again.

speaker: interviewee
text: And at that time, I-- the Polish soldiers were coming back from Russia. And one day I had-- that was in Zdulbunow. I had a feeling that my brother somehow survived.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I went to the-- to this railroad station. And I was looking. And all of a sudden a-- a soldier comes over, taps me on my shoulder. And he says, what's your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said, I don't talk to soldiers. I was very religious at that time. He said, you'd better talk, because you might have a brother here. And I turned around, and there was my brother, Joseph, Joseph standing. And that was a miracle so far away from home in a strange city. And I just had this intuition. And this is how I found my brother. But our reunion was only about like five minutes. And he asked me my-- about my parents. And I told him the story. It was-- it was very sad. And he gave me his platoon number, where he's going to be. And he left. And I-- later on, I was hitchhiking. [DRINKS] I was hitchhiking, and I found him. And I spend a little time-- not much. And he had to go, because he was Polish. So they took him to Poland. They were going through. And-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] and then I-- I was going with my future husband. And in six months, in 1944 in July, I married him. And he was 10 years older than I was. And then--

speaker: interviewer
text: Had he been in hiding during the war also?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was in the partisans. He was in the partisans. I remember when we even married, he still had guns with him. And [SIGHS] we lived there in Zdulbunow for a while in our apartment. And then the Russians were there. And we could have gone to Poland, because we were-- because we were Polish citizen. But little did we know that we are not welcome in Poland. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, the war had ended at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. The war was still on.

speaker: interviewer
text: What time are we talking about?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in 1944. Because the [? Fuhrer-- ?] over there where I was, there was no war. But the war was still on, the World War II. The war ended, what, in May, 1945?

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was 1944. And [SIGHS] when we came, we went to Radom. And we came-- we were staying there in a house. And the [INAUDIBLE] came to the window. And they were knocking at the window. And they-- and they said if the Jews are here? And at that time, I was speaking very good Polish. And I told them that we are Polish, not Jewish. Somehow, they believed. I just believe it was meant to be. Because that night, they killed eight Jewish women. Terrible-- with a saw. And they killed lots of Jewish people, so we just run. We were trying to-- to-- and that was already-- I think at that time must have been already the-- because we went in '45-- that was forty-- when we left there, it was already-- I think it was '45. Because we went through the occupied Germany by the Americans. We didn't go. So easy, we had to bribe people to take us. They took us to Czechoslovakia. And from Czechoslovakia, they took us to-- to-- to Germany, to Landsberg, Landsberg am Lech to the displaced-- to the displaced DP camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you in May of 1945 when the war ended?

speaker: interviewee
text: When-- I-- I was still in-- let's see-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was-- June-- I think that I was already in Landsberg.
